quiz.title: SAT English: Unit 2 Quiz 12
quiz.subtitle: Mixed Practice Across All Evidence Skills (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation

questions.question:
A historian argues that the spread of printing technology accelerated scientific progress more by enabling rapid peer review than by increasing public literacy.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Region} & \text{Printing Presses (per 100k)} & \text{Scientific Publications/year} \\ \hline\text{Region X} & 2.1 & 14 \\ \hline\text{Region Y} & 5.8 & 47 \\ \hline\text{Region Z} & 12.3 & 132 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this argument?
options: Public literacy rates increased steadily across all regions regardless of printing press density.
Scientific publications in Region Z focused primarily on theoretical rather than applied research.
Regions with more printing presses showed faster citation cycles between scientific publications.
Printing costs declined by 40% following technological improvements in paper manufacturing.
questions.explanation: Choice C directly supports the peer-review mechanism by linking press density to faster scholarly communication cycles. Choice A addresses literacy, which the argument explicitly downplays as the primary driver. Choice B discusses research focus, not dissemination speed. Choice D examines production costs, irrelevant to peer-review acceleration.

questions.question:
Ecologists claim that restoring wetland habitats reduces coastal flooding damage primarily by absorbing storm surge, not by acting as a physical barrier to wind.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Site Type} & \text{Storm Surge Absorption (%)} & \text{Wind Speed Reduction (%)} \\ \hline\text{Restored Wetland} & 68 & 12 \\ \hline\text{Natural Wetland} & 74 & 15 \\ \hline\text{Developed Coastline} & 8 & 9 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which choice best describes how the data relates to the ecologists' claim?
options: Developed coastlines show slightly higher wind reduction than restored wetlands in some storms.
Coastal communities report subjective perceptions of safety after wetland restoration projects.
Wetland plants grow more rapidly in regions with higher annual rainfall and milder temperatures.
Restored and natural wetlands absorb most storm surge while reducing wind speed minimally.
questions.explanation: Choice D neutrally describes the parallel pattern of high surge absorption paired with low wind reduction, which students must evaluate against the proposed mechanism. Choice A focuses on a minor comparison that doesn't address the core mechanism. Choice B cites subjective perceptions, not physical evidence. Choice C introduces plant growth rates, irrelevant to flood mitigation.

questions.question:
Urban planners hypothesize that expanding municipal green spaces reduces resident stress levels by providing accessible environments for physiological recovery.

Which finding would provide the most relevant evidence for this hypothesis?
options: Cortisol levels decreased by 18% in residents living within 300 meters of parks compared to those living further away.
Property values in neighborhoods adjacent to parks increased by 12% following recent landscaping projects.
Municipal trees absorb approximately 48 pounds of carbon dioxide annually, contributing to cleaner air.
One community member reported feeling noticeably relaxed after walking through the central botanical garden.
questions.explanation: Choice A provides direct physiological data linking proximity to green spaces with reduced stress markers, directly testing the hypothesized mechanism. Choice B addresses economic impact, not stress physiology. Choice C discusses environmental benefits unrelated to human stress responses. Choice D relies on a single anecdote rather than systematic evidence.

questions.question:
Economists propose that remote work adoption reduces urban commercial real estate demand but increases suburban residential property values.

Which finding from the market analysis best supports this proposal?
options: Commercial property taxes declined in three major cities following the shift to remote work policies.
Urban office vacancy rates rose from 12% to 31% while suburban home prices increased 18% over the same period.
Suburban municipalities reported increased demand for public transit expansions to accommodate commuter patterns.
Remote workers spend an average of $2,400 annually on home office equipment and high-speed internet upgrades.
questions.explanation: Choice B directly evidences both predicted outcomes: rising urban commercial vacancy and increasing suburban residential values. Choice A addresses only one side (commercial) and focuses on taxes, not demand. Choice C discusses transit infrastructure, not property values. Choice D examines worker spending, not real estate market trends.

questions.question:
A cognitive scientist hypothesizes that spaced repetition improves long-term retention not by increasing total study time, but by optimizing the timing of memory reconsolidation.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Study Schedule} & \text{Total Time (min)} & \text{Retention Score (%)} \\ \hline\text{Massed (1 session)} & 60 & 42 \\ \hline\text{Spaced (3 sessions)} & 60 & 71 \\ \hline\text{Spaced (6 sessions)} & 60 & 88 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this hypothesis?
options: Participants who studied longer overall achieved higher retention regardless of schedule.
Retention scores rise with more frequent sessions despite identical total study time.
Memory reconsolidation occurs at fixed intervals that vary significantly between individuals.
Students prefer spaced schedules because they feel less mentally fatigued during study.
questions.explanation: Choice B directly supports the timing-optimization claim by showing improved retention with identical total time. Choice A contradicts the hypothesis by emphasizing total time over scheduling. Choice C introduces individual variability without linking it to the spacing effect. Choice D cites preference data, not retention outcomes.

questions.question: Researchers studying sleep and memory found that participants who slept eight hours after learning a new skill showed 40% better retention than those who stayed awake. The data suggest that sleep enhances memory consolidation, as evidenced by ________.
options: no difference in retention between groups who napped versus those who slept fully
higher baseline memory scores among participants assigned to the sleep condition
a 40% retention advantage for the sleep group compared to the wake group
improved mood ratings in the sleep group that correlated with retention scores
questions.explanation: Choice C directly completes the claim by restating the key quantitative finding (40% advantage) that evidences consolidation. Choice A introduces an untested comparison (nap vs. full sleep). Choice B suggests pre-existing group differences, which would weaken the causal claim. Choice D cites mood, a confounding variable not central to the consolidation mechanism.

questions.question:
Public health researchers claim that community vaccination campaigns achieve herd immunity faster when targeting high-transmission age groups first, rather than distributing doses evenly across all demographics.

Which outcome from the campaign evaluation best supports this claim?
options: Vaccine efficacy remained above 90% across all age groups, with no significant variation in immune response by demographic category.
Public trust in vaccination increased by 25% following community-led outreach programs in underserved neighborhoods.
The cost per dose decreased by 15% when vaccines were administered through mobile clinics rather than fixed facilities.
Regions prioritizing adolescents achieved herd immunity thresholds with 42% coverage, while regions using even distribution required 68% coverage.
questions.explanation: Choice D directly supports the strategic-targeting claim by showing lower overall coverage achieved herd immunity faster when high-transmission groups were prioritized. Choice A addresses vaccine efficacy, not distribution strategy. Choice B discusses public trust, not transmission dynamics. Choice C focuses on logistical costs, not epidemiological outcomes.

questions.question:
Linguists propose that language change accelerates in communities with high rates of adult second-language acquisition because learners simplify complex grammatical structures, which then spread to native speakers.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Community} & \text{Adult L2 Learners (%)} & \text{Grammatical Simplification Index} \\ \hline\text{Alpha} & 12 & 2.1 \\ \hline\text{Beta} & 38 & 4.7 \\ \hline\text{Gamma} & 61 & 7.9 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which choice best describes how the data relates to the linguists' proposal?
options: Grammatical simplification scores rise as the percentage of adult L2 learners increases across communities.
Communities with greater immigrant populations showed faster lexical innovation but no change in syntactic complexity.
Second-language learners consistently mastered phonological patterns more readily than morphological inflections.
Languages with more complex case systems were acquired more slowly by adult learners than languages with fixed word order.
questions.explanation: Choice A neutrally describes the positive correlation between L2 learner percentage and simplification index, which students must evaluate against the proposed transmission mechanism. Choice B addresses lexical change, not grammatical simplification. Choice C describes acquisition patterns without linking them to community-level change. Choice D compares language features without addressing the spread of simplifications.

questions.question:
Nutritionists claim that replacing red meat with plant-based proteins reduces systemic inflammation by lowering saturated fat intake and increasing antioxidant consumption.

Which finding from the clinical trial best supports this claim?
options: Industrial livestock production generates significant methane emissions, accelerating climate change.
Plant-based groceries often cost 15% more than conventional meat products at suburban supermarkets.
Many individuals report disliking the texture of lentils and prefer traditional beef preparations.
Blood tests revealed a 22% reduction in inflammatory markers among participants who replaced red meat with legumes for eight weeks.
questions.explanation: Choice D provides clinical biomarker data directly measuring the claimed physiological mechanism (reduced inflammation). Choice A addresses environmental impact, not human health outcomes. Choice B discusses market pricing, irrelevant to biological effects. Choice C cites personal taste preferences, not scientific evidence.

questions.question:
Historians claim that the expansion of public education in the 19th century accelerated industrial growth primarily by creating a literate workforce, not by fostering civic engagement.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Region} & \text{Literacy Rate (%)} & \text{Factory Employment Growth (%)} \\ \hline\text{Region A} & 42 & 18 \\ \hline\text{Region B} & 67 & 54 \\ \hline\text{Region C} & 81 & 89 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this claim?
options: Industrial output correlated more strongly with access to natural resources than with education levels across all regions studied.
Literacy rates and factory employment growth rose in parallel across all three regions studied.
Civic participation metrics increased significantly in regions with the earliest public-school adoption.
Regions with higher literacy rates showed greater political stability during periods of economic change.
questions.explanation: Choice C challenges the literate-workforce mechanism by showing civic engagement rising alongside education, suggesting an alternative pathway for industrial growth that the claim downplays. Choice A introduces resource access, a confounding variable not central to the education claim. Choice B directly supports the proposed mechanism. Choice D addresses political stability, a related civic outcome but doesn't directly link to industrial growth.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can accurately evaluate evidence across scientific and social-science contexts by distinguishing mechanism-specific support from true-but-irrelevant data.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on whether the evidence addresses the exact causal pathway stated in the claim, not just a related topic.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: eliminate options that describe plausible phenomena but don't isolate the specific mechanism being tested.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Ask yourself: does this finding prove or disprove the precise causal relationship the researchers are proposing?
